verses.flow: The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, Son of God.
verses.note: GREEK: "Archē" (G746) means "beginning" or "origin," echoing Genesis 1:1 (LXX). "Euangelion" (G2098) was used in the Roman world for imperial proclamations of victory or a new emperor's accession. Mark applies this politically charged term to Jesus, declaring him as the true sovereign. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "Son of God" (textually disputed but attested in major manuscripts including Sinaiticus corrector, Vaticanus) establishes Jesus's divine identity from the opening line — he is not merely the Christ, but the Son of God who shares in the divine nature.

verses.flow: Even as it has been written in Isaiah the prophet: "Behold, I send my messenger before your face, who shall prepare your way,"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Mark attributes the composite quotation to Isaiah, though v2b draws from Malachi 3:1 (and possibly Exodus 23:20), while v3 comes from Isaiah 40:3. The Malachi passage concerns God sending his messenger before himself — Mark applies this to John preparing the way for Jesus, implying Jesus occupies the role of YHWH in the original prophecy. GREEK: "Kataskeuasei" (G2680) means "to prepare, construct, make ready" — the messenger's task is active preparation of the road for the coming King.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: "A voice of one crying in the wilderness: Prepare the way of the Lord, make straight his paths."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Isaiah 40:3 in its original context announces YHWH's return to Zion through the wilderness. Mark applies "Lord" (kyrios, G2962) to Jesus — the one whose way John prepares is identified with the Lord God of Israel. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The voice crying in the wilderness recalls the heavenly herald announcing divine action; the wilderness motif connects to Israel's exodus and the expectation of a new exodus under God's direct kingship.

verses.flow: And he was proclaiming, saying, "There comes the one mightier than I after me, of whom I am not worthy, having stooped down, to loose the strap of his sandals."
"I baptized you with water, but he shall baptize you in the Holy Spirit."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between water baptism and Spirit baptism marks the eschatological transition. John's baptism signifies repentance; Jesus's baptism in the Holy Spirit (pneumati hagiō, G4151 + G40) signifies the new covenant reality prophesied in Joel 2:28-29 and Ezekiel 36:26-27. The coming one does what only God can do — pour out the Spirit.

verses.flow: And it came to pass in those days that Jesus came from Nazareth of Galilee and was baptized in the Jordan by John.

verses.flow: And immediately, coming up out of the water, he saw the heavens being torn open and the Spirit as a dove descending upon him.
verses.note: GREEK: "Schizomenous" (G4977) means "being torn, ripped apart" — Mark uses a violent participle, not merely "opened." This same root appears in Mark 15:38 when the temple veil is "torn" at Jesus's death, forming a literary inclusio. THEOLOGICAL: The tearing of the heavens fulfills Isaiah 64:1 ("Oh that you would tear the heavens and come down"). The barrier between heaven and earth is breached at Jesus's baptism. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Spirit descending and the heavenly voice together present a scene of the divine council commissioning the Son for his earthly mission.

verses.flow: And a voice came out of the heavens: "You are my Son, the Beloved; in you I am well pleased."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The heavenly voice combines Psalm 2:7 ("You are my Son") — a royal enthronement psalm — with Isaiah 42:1 ("my chosen one in whom my soul delights") — the Servant of the Lord. Jesus is simultaneously enthroned King and Suffering Servant. TYPOLOGY: The divine declaration at the Jordan parallels the anointing of Israel's kings, yet transcends it: this is not adoption language but the Father's eternal acknowledgment of the Son's identity and mission.

verses.flow: And immediately the Spirit drives him out into the wilderness.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he was in the wilderness forty days, being tempted by Satan, and he was with the wild beasts, and the angels were ministering to him.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The forty days echo Israel's forty years in the wilderness (Deut 8:2) and Moses's forty days on Sinai (Exod 34:28). Where Israel failed under testing, Jesus prevails. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Satan (G4567, "the Adversary") functions as the accuser from the divine council (cf. Job 1-2; Zech 3:1). The angels ministering (diēkonoun, G1247) to Jesus recalls Psalm 91:11-12 and portrays the faithful spiritual beings serving the Son, in contrast to the rebel adversary who tests him. The wild beasts may evoke the Edenic restoration theme (Isa 11:6-9).

verses.flow: And after John was delivered up, Jesus came into Galilee, proclaiming the gospel of God,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and saying, "The time has been fulfilled, and the kingdom of God has drawn near. Repent and believe in the gospel."
verses.note: GREEK: "Peplērōtai" (G4137) is a perfect passive — "has been fulfilled," indicating a completed divine action with ongoing results. "Kairos" (G2540) is not mere chronological time (chronos) but the divinely appointed, decisive moment. THEOLOGICAL: The kingdom of God (basileia tou theou) denotes God's active, sovereign reign breaking into the present age. The dual command "repent and believe" summarizes the proper human response to God's eschatological action in Christ.

verses.flow: And they were astonished at his teaching, for he was teaching them as one having authority, and not as the scribes.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The astonishment (exeplēssonto, G1605, "struck out of their senses") at Jesus's teaching reveals the unprecedented nature of his authority (exousia, G1849). Unlike the scribes who derived authority from tradition and citation of previous teachers, Jesus teaches as one possessing inherent authority — the authority of God himself. HISTORICAL: Scribes (grammateis, G1122) were the professional Torah scholars of Second Temple Judaism, experts in interpreting and transmitting the oral and written law.

verses.flow: And immediately there was in their synagogue a man with an unclean spirit, and he cried out,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: saying, "What is there between us and you, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are — the Holy One of God!"
verses.note: GREEK: "Ti hēmin kai soi" is a Semitic idiom meaning "What have we to do with you?" — expressing a desire for separation and non-involvement. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The unclean spirit (pneuma akatharton) recognizes Jesus's true identity as "the Holy One of God" (ho hagios tou theou) — a title linking Jesus to divine holiness and the consecrated role of God's anointed agent. The demons' supernatural knowledge of Jesus contrasts with human ignorance, a key theme in Mark's "messianic secret." The plural "us" may refer to the entire demonic realm that Jesus has come to overthrow.

And the report of him went out immediately everywhere into all the surrounding region of Galilee.

verses.flow: And he healed many who were ill with various diseases, and he cast out many demons, and he was not permitting the demons to speak, because they knew him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus silences the demons despite their correct identification of him. This "messianic secret" motif in Mark indicates that Jesus's identity cannot be rightly understood apart from the cross. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The demons' knowledge of Jesus ("they knew him") reflects the spiritual realm's awareness of the divine Son's true identity and authority. Their forced obedience demonstrates that the kingdom of God is actively overthrowing the dominion of hostile spiritual powers.

verses.flow: And rising up very early, while it was still dark, he went out and departed to a desolate place, and there he was praying.
And Simon and those with him pursued after him,
and they found him and say to him, "All are seeking you."

verses.flow: And he says to them, "Let us go elsewhere, into the neighboring towns, so that I may proclaim there also, for it was for this purpose that I came forth."
verses.note: GREEK: "Exēlthon" (G1831) — "I came forth" — carries theological weight beyond geographical movement. Jesus's mission originates from the Father; he "came forth" with divine purpose. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus refuses to be defined by popular demand. His mission is proclamation (kēryssō, G2784) of the kingdom, not merely miracle-working. The priority of the word over signs establishes the pattern for apostolic ministry.

verses.flow: And a leper comes to him, imploring him and kneeling before him, saying to him, "If you are willing, you are able to cleanse me."

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And being moved with compassion, having stretched out his hand, he touched him and says to him, "I am willing; be cleansed."
verses.note: GREEK: Some early manuscripts read "orgistheis" (G3710, "being angered") instead of "splanchnistheis" ("being moved with compassion"). If original, Jesus's anger may be directed at the disease itself, or at the ravaging effects of sin and death in creation. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus's touching of the leper is extraordinary — contact with a leper rendered one ceremonially unclean (Lev 13:45-46). Instead of Jesus becoming unclean, his holiness and power reverse the flow: the unclean is made clean. This prefigures the new covenant reality where God's holiness is not defiled by contact with sinners but rather purifies them.

verses.flow: And immediately the leprosy departed from him, and he was cleansed.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having sternly warned him, immediately he sent him away,
and says to him, "See that you say nothing to anyone, but go, show yourself to the priest, and offer concerning your cleansing what Moses commanded, for a testimony to them."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The command to show himself to the priest and offer the prescribed sacrifice follows Leviticus 14:1-32, the detailed ritual for cleansing from leprosy. "For a testimony to them" (eis martyrion autois) may mean as evidence to the priests that messianic healing has begun, or as a witness against their unbelief. TYPOLOGY: Jesus upholds the Mosaic law while simultaneously demonstrating authority that transcends it — he heals what only God can heal, then directs the healed man back to the Torah's prescribed order.

verses.flow: But having gone out, he began to proclaim many things and to spread abroad the word, so that he was no longer able to enter openly into a city, but was outside in desolate places; and they were coming to him from every direction.

And many were gathered together, so that there was no longer room, not even at the door; and He was speaking to them the word.

verses.flow: And they come, bringing to Him a paralytic, being carried by four men.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And not being able to bring him near on account of the crowd, they uncovered the roof where He was, and having dug through it, they let down the mat on which the paralytic was lying.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And Jesus, having seen their faith, says to the paralytic, "Child, your sins are forgiven."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' declaration "your sins are forgiven" (ἀφίενταί σου αἱ ἁμαρτίαι) uses the divine passive, implying God as the agent of forgiveness. Yet Jesus speaks this on His own authority, which the scribes immediately recognize as a divine prerogative claim. The word τέκνον (teknon, "child") conveys tender affection beyond mere address. TYPOLOGY: This foreshadows the ultimate forgiveness accomplished at the cross (Isa 53:5; Col 2:13-14).

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But certain of the scribes were sitting there and reasoning in their hearts,
"Why does this one speak thus? He blasphemes! Who is able to forgive sins except one—God?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The scribes' reasoning is theologically correct in premise—only God can forgive sins (Isa 43:25; Ps 103:3)—but catastrophically wrong in conclusion. Their own logic, properly followed, should have led them to recognize Jesus' divine identity. GREEK: βλασφημεῖ (blasphēmeí, Strong's G987) means to speak injuriously against God; the charge carried a death penalty under Torah (Lev 24:16).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And immediately Jesus, having known in His spirit that they were reasoning thus within themselves, says to them, "Why do you reason these things in your hearts?
Which is easier, to say to the paralytic, 'Your sins are forgiven,' or to say, 'Rise, and take up your mat, and walk'?
But that you may know that the Son of Man has authority upon the earth to forgive sins"—He says to the paralytic—
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Son of Man" (ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου) is Jesus' primary self-designation, drawn from Daniel 7:13-14, where the Son of Man receives dominion, glory, and kingdom from the Ancient of Days. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The claim of ἐξουσία (exousía, "authority," Strong's G1849) to forgive sins "upon the earth" implies that heavenly authority is now operative on earth through the Son of Man—a direct echo of the Dan 7 enthronement scene. The healing serves as visible proof of the invisible forgiveness.

verses.flow: "To you I say, rise, take up your mat, and go to your house."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: And he rose, and immediately having taken up the mat, he went out before them all, so that all were amazed and glorified God, saying, "Never have we seen anything like this!"
And He went out again beside the sea, and all the crowd was coming to Him, and He was teaching them.

verses.flow: And passing by, He saw Levi the son of Alphaeus sitting at the tax booth, and He says to him, "Follow Me." And having risen, he followed Him.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Levi son of Alphaeus is generally identified with Matthew the apostle (Matt 9:9). The τελώνιον (telṓnion, "tax booth," Strong's G5058) was a customs post likely on the Via Maris trade route near Capernaum. Tax collectors (τελῶναι) served Rome's interests and were despised as collaborators and ritually unclean by association with Gentiles. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' call of a tax collector demonstrates that the Kingdom breaks through social and religious boundaries of Second Temple purity expectations.

verses.flow: And it came to pass that He was reclining at table in his house, and many tax collectors and sinners were reclining together with Jesus and His disciples, for they were many, and they were following Him.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And the scribes of the Pharisees, having seen that He eats with sinners and tax collectors, were saying to His disciples, "Why does He eat with tax collectors and sinners?"

verses.flow: And having heard this, Jesus says to them, "Those who are strong have no need of a physician, but those who are ill. I came not to call the righteous, but sinners."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' physician metaphor reveals the mission of the Messiah: not to affirm the self-righteous but to bring healing to those who acknowledge their spiritual sickness. GREEK: δικαίους (dikaíous, "righteous," Strong's G1342) here carries ironic force—those who consider themselves righteous exclude themselves from the very salvation Jesus offers. This anticipates the Pauline doctrine that none are righteous in themselves (Rom 3:10, citing Ps 14:1-3).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And the disciples of John and the Pharisees were fasting. And they come and say to Him, "Why do the disciples of John and the disciples of the Pharisees fast, but Your disciples do not fast?"
And Jesus said to them, "Are the sons of the bridechamber able to fast while the bridegroom is with them? As long as they have the bridegroom with them, they are not able to fast.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The bridegroom (νυμφίος, nymphíos, Strong's G3566) imagery is a direct messianic claim. In the OT, YHWH is Israel's bridegroom (Isa 62:5; Hos 2:16-20). Jesus applies this title to Himself, identifying His presence with the eschatological wedding feast. The "sons of the bridechamber" (οἱ υἱοὶ τοῦ νυμφῶνος) is a Semitic idiom for wedding guests or attendants of the groom.

verses.flow: But days will come when the bridegroom shall be taken away from them, and then they will fast in that day.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "taken away" (ἀπαρθῇ, aparthē, from ἀπαίρω, Strong's G522) is the first veiled prediction of Jesus' violent death in Mark's Gospel. The verb carries connotations of forcible removal. This transforms fasting from mere ritual observance into an eschatological marker: mourning is appropriate only when the bridegroom is absent. TYPOLOGY: Echoes the Suffering Servant who is "cut off from the land of the living" (Isa 53:8).

words.g: which is put in to fill up

verses.flow: No one sews a patch of unshrunk cloth upon an old garment; but if so, the fullness takes away from it—the new from the old—and a worse tear results.
And no one puts new wine into old wineskins; but if so, the wine will burst the wineskins, and the wine is destroyed, and the wineskins. But new wine is for fresh wineskins."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The twin parables of the unshrunk cloth and new wine declare that the kingdom Jesus brings cannot be contained within the existing structures of Second Temple Judaism. GREEK: νέον (néon) refers to wine "new" in time/age, while καινούς (kainoús) for the wineskins means "new" in quality or kind—a subtle distinction suggesting the eschatological newness of the kingdom (cf. 2 Cor 5:17). This is not mere reform but new creation.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And it came to pass that He was going through the grain fields on the Sabbath, and His disciples began to make their way, plucking the heads of grain.
And the Pharisees were saying to Him, "Look, why do they do on the Sabbath what is not lawful?"
And He says to them, "Have you never read what David did when he had need and was hungry, he and those with him?

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: How he entered into the house of God in the time of Abiathar the high priest, and ate the bread of the Presence, which is not lawful to eat except for the priests, and gave also to those who were with him?"
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The reference to Abiathar (Ἀβιαθάρ) as high priest presents a well-known difficulty, since 1 Samuel 21:1-6 names Ahimelech as the priest who gave David the bread. The Greek ἐπὶ Ἀβιαθὰρ ἀρχιερέως may mean "in the section concerning Abiathar" or "in the time of Abiathar" (who became the prominent high priest shortly after). TYPOLOGY: The bread of the Presence (ἄρτους τῆς προθέσεως, cf. Exod 25:30; Lev 24:5-9) typologically anticipates the Eucharistic bread—the true Presence of God given to those in need.

verses.flow: And He was saying to them, "The Sabbath was made for man, and not man for the Sabbath.
So then, the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The climactic declaration that the Son of Man is κύριος (kýrios, "Lord," Strong's G2962) even of the Sabbath is a staggering authority claim. Since God instituted the Sabbath at creation (Gen 2:2-3) and commanded its observance (Exod 20:8-11), lordship over the Sabbath implies lordship held by the Creator Himself. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This verse, read alongside v.10, establishes a pattern in Mark 2: the Son of Man possesses divine prerogatives—forgiving sins (v.10) and sovereign authority over the Sabbath (v.28)—that belong to God alone in the OT framework.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular feminine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And He entered again into the synagogue, and a man was there having the hand withered.
And they were watching Him closely, whether on the Sabbath He would heal him, so that they might accuse Him.
verses.note: GREEK: παρετήρουν (paratēreō, Strong's G3906) is imperfect tense, indicating continuous, hostile observation — "they kept watching closely." This verb carries connotations of malicious surveillance, not innocent observation. The Pharisees had already predetermined their verdict.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: And He says to the man having the withered hand, "Rise up into the midst."
And He says to them, "Is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good or to do evil, to save a soul or to kill?" But they were silent.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus frames the Sabbath question as a binary moral imperative: to do good (ἀγαθοποιῆσαι) or to do evil (κακοποιῆσαι), to save a soul (ψυχὴν σῶσαι) or to kill (ἀποκτεῖναι). His logic reveals that inaction in the face of suffering is itself evil. The irony is devastating: while Jesus heals on the Sabbath, the Pharisees plot murder (v. 6).

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having looked around at them with anger, being grieved at the hardness of their heart, He says to the man, "Stretch out your hand." And he stretched it out, and his hand was restored.
verses.note: GREEK: πωρώσει (pōrōsis, Strong's G4457) means "hardness, petrification" — a calcification of the heart. This rare noun (also in Rom 11:25; Eph 4:18) conveys spiritual ossification beyond mere stubbornness. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus displays both divine anger (ὀργῆς) and compassionate grief (συλλυπούμενος) simultaneously — righteous wrath and sorrow coexisting, revealing the full emotional range of God incarnate.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And having gone out, the Pharisees immediately were taking counsel with the Herodians against Him, so that they might destroy Him.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Pharisees and Herodians were natural enemies — the Pharisees resisted Hellenizing influence while the Herodians supported the Herodian dynasty and its Roman patronage. Their conspiracy against Jesus (cf. Mark 12:13) reveals that opposing factions unite when threatened by the kingdom of God. This alliance foreshadows the coalition that will ultimately crucify Him.

verses.flow: And Jesus with His disciples withdrew to the sea, and a great multitude from Galilee followed, and from Judea,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and from Jerusalem, and from Idumea, and beyond the Jordan, and around Tyre and Sidon — a great multitude, hearing how many things He was doing, came to Him.
And He spoke to His disciples that a small boat should wait ready for Him on account of the crowd, lest they should press upon Him.
For He healed many, so that as many as had afflictions were falling upon Him in order that they might touch Him.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: And the unclean spirits, whenever they beheld Him, were falling down before Him and were crying out, saying, "You are the Son of God!"
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The unclean spirits (πνεύματα ἀκάθαρτα) recognize Jesus as "the Son of God" (ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ) — a title with divine council significance. These rebellious spiritual beings recognize the identity that humans fail to perceive. Their forced prostration (προσέπιπτον) before Jesus demonstrates His sovereign authority over the spiritual realm (cf. Ps 82; Phil 2:10).

verses.flow: And He was sternly warning them many times that they should not make Him known.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' stern silencing (ἐπετίμα, Strong's G2008) of the demons is part of Mark's "Messianic Secret" motif. Though the demons' confession is factually true, Jesus will not receive His identity proclamation from unclean spirits. His messiahship must be revealed on His own terms — ultimately through the cross (Mark 15:39).

verses.flow: And He goes up onto the mountain and summons those whom He Himself desired, and they went away to Him.
And He appointed twelve, that they might be with Him, and that He might send them out to proclaim,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The appointment of twelve (δώδεκα) deliberately echoes the twelve tribes of Israel. Jesus, ascending the mountain (v. 13) like Moses at Sinai, constitutes a renewed Israel around Himself. The verb ἐποίησεν ("He made/appointed") carries creative force — He is not merely selecting but constituting a new covenant community (cf. Ex 18:25; 1 Kgs 12:31).

verses.flow: and to have authority to cast out the demons.
And He appointed the twelve: and He bestowed upon Simon the name Peter,
and James the son of Zebedee, and John the brother of James (and He bestowed upon them the name Boanerges, which is, Sons of Thunder),
and Andrew, and Philip, and Bartholomew, and Matthew, and Thomas, and James the son of Alphaeus, and Thaddaeus, and Simon the Zealot,
and Judas Iscariot, who also betrayed Him.

This has profound implications for the new covenant community — membership in God's family is constituted by doing, not by bloodline (cf. John 1:12-13; Rom 8:14-17).

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The image of birds nesting in branches echoes Daniel 4:12 and Ezekiel 17:23; 31:6 LXX, where a great tree sheltering birds symbolizes a vast kingdom encompassing the nations. The mustard seed parable thus envisions the kingdom of God growing to encompass all peoples. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "birds of the heaven" (τὰ πετεινὰ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ) in prophetic imagery often represent the nations coming under God's sovereign rule.

verses.flow: And having been awakened, he rebuked the wind and said to the sea, "Be silent! Be muzzled!" And the wind ceased, and there came a great calm.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus rebukes (epitimáō, ἐπετίμησεν) the wind—the same verb used for rebuking demons (Mark 1:25; 3:12). "Phimóō" (πεφίμωσο, "be muzzled") is also used in the exorcism of Mark 1:25, suggesting cosmic authority over chaotic forces. TYPOLOGY: This scene echoes Psalm 89:9 (LXX 88:10) and Psalm 107:29 where God alone stills the sea and its waves—an unmistakable claim to divine identity. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Mastery over sea and storm is exclusively a divine prerogative in the Old Testament (Job 38:8-11; Ps 65:7; 104:7; Isa 51:15).

verses.flow: And he said to them, "Why are you fearful? Do you not yet have faith?"
And they feared with a great fear and were saying to one another, "Who then is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?"
verses.note: GREEK: "Ephobēthēsan phobon megan" (ἐφοβήθησαν φόβον μέγαν)—"they feared a great fear"—is a Semitic cognate accusative construction intensifying the verb. THEOLOGICAL: The disciples' question "Tis ara houtos estin" ("Who then is this?") is the central Christological question of Mark's Gospel. That wind and sea "obey" (hypakoúei) him points to his divine identity, since in the Old Testament only YHWH commands the chaotic waters (Ps 33:7; 104:7; Nah 1:4). The expected answer: this is the God of Israel incarnate.

verses.flow: And they came to the other side of the sea, into the region of the Gerasenes.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "region of the Gerasenes" (Γερασηνῶν) places Jesus in predominantly Gentile territory east of the Sea of Galilee, part of the Decapolis. Textual variants include "Gadarenes" and "Gergesenes." This crossing into pagan land is theologically significant — the Lord of all extends his authority beyond Israel.

The preposition ἐν indicates the sphere of influence under which the man existed. The verb ὁρκίζω (horkizō, Strong's G3726) means "I adjure/place under oath," an ironic reversal where the demonic attempts to invoke divine authority against the Divine Son himself.

HISTORICAL: The use of a Roman military term for a demonic host in occupied territory carries overtones of imperial oppression — the man is occupied by a foreign legion, mirroring Israel's own subjugation. The shift from singular to plural ("we are many") reveals the corporate nature of the possession.

verses.flow: And having taken hold of the hand of the child, he says to her, "Talitha koum," which is, being interpreted, "Little girl, I say to you, arise."

verses.flow: And Jesus said to them, "A prophet is not without honor except in his hometown and among his relatives and in his household."

verses.flow: And having seen them straining at the rowing — for the wind was against them — about the fourth watch of the night, He comes to them walking upon the sea. And He was wanting to pass by them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Walking upon the sea" (peripatōn epi tēs thalassēs) evokes Job 9:8 LXX where God alone "walks upon the sea" and Psalm 77:19. The phrase "He was wanting to pass by them" (ēthelen parelthein) echoes the divine theophanies of Exodus 33:19-22 and 1 Kings 19:11, where YHWH "passes by." This is a deliberate divine self-revelation — Jesus acts in the identity of YHWH. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The sea in ancient Near Eastern cosmology represents chaos; to walk upon it is to exercise sovereign dominion over the forces of chaos, an act reserved for the Creator God alone.

verses.flow: But they, having seen Him walking upon the sea, thought that it was a phantom, and they cried out.
For they all saw Him and were troubled. But immediately He spoke with them and says to them, "Take courage! I am. Fear not."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Egō eimi" (ἐγώ εἰμι) — "I am" — functions here both as simple self-identification and as a theophanic declaration echoing the divine Name of Exodus 3:14 (LXX: egō eimi ho ōn). Combined with walking on the sea and the "passing by" motif, this constitutes one of the most explicit divine identity claims in Mark's Gospel. The command "Fear not" (mē phobeisthe) is the characteristic divine reassurance in Old Testament theophanies (Isa 41:10, 43:1).

STRUCTURE: This forms a thematic bracket with Mark 8:17-21 where Jesus will directly confront their failure to understand the loaves.

verses.note: GREEK: The word πυγμῇ (pygmē, "with the fist") is unusual and debated—it likely refers to a specific manner of ritual handwashing, perhaps rubbing the fist into the open palm. Some take it as "diligently" or "up to the wrist." This parenthetical explanation (vv. 3–4) is Mark's editorial note for his Gentile audience unfamiliar with Jewish purity customs. HISTORICAL: The "tradition of the elders" (παράδοσιν τῶν πρεσβυτέρων) refers to the Oral Torah, later codified in the Mishnah, which Pharisees considered binding alongside written Scripture.

The word ξεστῶν (xestōn) is a loanword from Latin sextarius, a liquid measure vessel—a rare borrowing indicating Roman cultural influence. HISTORICAL: These extensive purity regulations reflect Second Temple-era expansion of priestly holiness codes (Lev 11–15) to ordinary daily life, a distinctly Pharisaic program.

THEOLOGICAL: The term ὑποκριτῶν (hypokritōn, "hypocrites," Strong's G5273) originally denoted a stage actor; Jesus applies it to those whose outward religious performance masks inward distance from God. The citation form ὡς γέγραπται ("as it is written") is a standard authoritative formula for Scripture.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Peter's confession "Su ei ho Christos" — "You are the Christ" — is the climactic turning point of Mark's Gospel. "Christos" (Χριστός, G5547) translates Hebrew "Mashiach" (Anointed One), the promised Davidic King (2 Sam 7:12-16; Ps 2:2; Dan 9:25-26). Peter rightly identifies Jesus's identity but, as vv. 32-33 will reveal, profoundly misunderstands the nature of His messianic mission.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is the first of three Passion predictions in Mark (8:31; 9:31; 10:33-34). "Dei" (δεῖ, G1163) — "it is necessary" — indicates divine necessity, not mere human fate. The Son of Man "must" suffer according to the determined counsel of God. TYPOLOGY: "The Son of Man" (ho huios tou anthrōpou) draws from Daniel 7:13-14, where the figure receives an everlasting kingdom — yet Jesus radically redefines this through the path of suffering, merging Daniel's exalted figure with the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Hypage opisō mou, Satana" — "Get behind me, Satan!" Jesus identifies Peter's rebuke as satanic opposition to the divine plan. "Satanas" (Σατανᾶς, G4567), transliterated from Hebrew "ha-satan," designates the adversary. Peter unwittingly assumes the role of the accuser by opposing God's redemptive purpose through the cross. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The cosmic conflict surfaces here — Satan's strategy, whether through direct temptation (1:13) or through a well-meaning disciple, is to divert the Messiah from the cross.

The irony is profound: Peter who refuses to accept a suffering Messiah will himself fail at the very self-denial Jesus here requires. THEOLOGICAL: The cross (stauros, G4716) was not yet a religious symbol but an instrument of Roman execution and public shame — Jesus's call is radically literal.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Son of Man" who must suffer (v. 31) will also come "in the glory of his Father with the holy angels" — a clear allusion to Daniel 7:13-14 and the divine council assembly. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "holy angels" (tōn angelōn tōn hagiōn) constitute the heavenly host — members of the divine council (cf. Deut 33:2; Ps 89:5-7; Zech 14:5). The Son of Man arrives not merely as a human figure but as the one who shares the Father's glory and commands the heavenly armies, exercising the prerogative of divine judgment.

And Jesus said to them, "The things of Caesar render to Caesar, and the things of God to God." And they were utterly amazed at him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: ἀπόδοτε (apodote, G591) means "give back" or "render" — implying that what bears Caesar's image belongs to Caesar, but what bears God's image (humanity itself, Gen 1:26-27) belongs to God. The statement transcends a mere political answer, making a radical claim about divine ownership of all persons. HISTORICAL: The κῆνσος (kēnsos, G2778) was the Roman poll-tax, deeply resented in Judea and the catalyst for Judas the Galilean's revolt in AD 6.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus quotes the Shema (Deut 6:4-5), the foundational confession of Israel's faith. κύριος ὁ θεὸς ἡμῶν κύριος εἷς ἐστιν — "The Lord our God, the Lord is one." STRUCTURE: The pairing of Deut 6:4-5 with Lev 19:18 (v.31) as the two greatest commandments forms the ethical-theological center of Mark's Gospel.

verses.note: GREEK: γενεά (genea, G1074) can mean "generation" (a contemporaneous group), "race/people," or "age." verses.flow: Heaven and earth shall pass away, but my words shall by no means pass away.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is of profound Christological significance. The Son (ὁ υἱός, ho huios) places himself above the angels in the hierarchy of knowledge yet voluntarily subordinate to the Father regarding the appointed time. This reflects the incarnate Son's voluntary limitation within his earthly mission (cf. Phil 2:6-8), not an ontological deficiency. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The ranking—neither angels in heaven, nor the Son, except the Father—mirrors the heavenly hierarchy and underscores the Father's unique prerogative over eschatological timing.

verses.flow: And having entered into the tomb, they saw a young man sitting on the right side, clothed in a white robe, and they were greatly amazed.
verses.note: GREEK: "Neaniskon" (νεανίσκον, G3495) means "young man," yet the white robe (stolēn leukēn) and the reaction of astonishment (exethambēthēsan, G1568) indicate an angelic figure. Matthew 28:2-5 explicitly identifies this figure as an angel. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Angels as heavenly messengers clothed in white appear throughout Scripture as members of God's divine council dispatched to announce his decrees (cf. Dan 10:5-6; Acts 1:10).

verses.flow: But he says to them, "Do not be amazed. You seek Jesus the Nazarene, the one who was crucified. He has been raised; he is not here. Behold, the place where they laid him."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The passive "ēgerthē" (ἠγέρθη, G1453) — "he has been raised" — is a divine passive, indicating God the Father as the agent of resurrection. This is the climactic declaration of Mark's Gospel: the crucified Nazarene lives. TYPOLOGY: The empty tomb fulfills Jesus' own threefold passion predictions (Mark 8:31; 9:31; 10:34) and echoes the pattern of divine deliverance from Sheol in the Psalms (Ps 16:10).

verses.flow: "But go, tell his disciples and Peter that he goes before you into Galilee; there you will see him, just as he told you."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The singling out of Peter ("and Peter") after his threefold denial (Mark 14:66-72) reveals a deliberate message of restoration and grace. Jesus does not abandon the one who denied him. GREEK: "Proagei" (προάγει, G4254), "he goes before," is a present tense indicating Jesus as the shepherd who leads his flock (cf. Mark 14:28; John 10:4).

verses.flow: And having gone out, they fled from the tomb, for trembling and astonishment held them; and they said nothing to anyone, for they were afraid. ⟦And all the things commanded they reported briefly to those around Peter. And after these things, Jesus himself also, from east to west, sent out through them the sacred and imperishable proclamation of eternal salvation. Amen.⟧
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Many scholars regard verse 8 as the original ending of Mark's Gospel, with "ephobounto gar" (ἐφοβοῦντο γάρ, "for they were afraid") forming a strikingly abrupt conclusion. The shorter ending in brackets (⟦...⟧) appears in a few later manuscripts but is widely considered a later scribal addition to soften this abruptness. GREEK: "Tromos" (τρόμος, G5156) and "ekstasis" (ἔκστασις, G1611) together convey trembling and a state of being beside oneself — the proper human response to divine theophany.

verses.flow: ⟦Now having risen early on the first day of the week, he appeared first to Mary the Magdalene, from whom he had cast out seven demons.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Verses 9-20 constitute the so-called "Longer Ending of Mark," absent from the earliest and most reliable manuscripts (Codex Sinaiticus, Codex Vaticanus) and noted as secondary by early Church fathers including Eusebius and Jerome. Nevertheless, the passage was received into the canonical tradition at an early date. GREEK: "Anastas" (ἀναστάς, G450) — "having risen" — is the same resurrection vocabulary used throughout the NT kerygma.

verses.flow: And he said to them, "Having gone into all the world, proclaim the gospel to all creation.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Great Commission in Mark's form uses "ktisis" (κτίσις, G2937), meaning "creation" — the gospel is to be proclaimed not merely to all nations but to "all creation," reflecting the cosmic scope of Christ's lordship and redemption (cf. Rom 8:19-22; Col 1:15-20). GREEK: "Kēryxate" (κηρύξατε, G2784) is an aorist imperative — a decisive, urgent command to herald the good news as a royal proclamation.

verses.flow: "And these signs will accompany those who have believed: in my name they will cast out demons; they will speak in new tongues;
"they will take up serpents; and if they drink anything deadly, it will by no means harm them; upon the sick they will lay hands, and they will be well."
The Lord Jesus then, after he had spoken to them, was taken up into heaven and sat down at the right hand of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' session "at the right hand of God" (ek dexiōn tou theou) is a direct fulfillment of Psalm 110:1 (LXX Ps 109:1): "Sit at my right hand." This is the most frequently cited OT text in the NT, establishing Christ's sovereign enthronement as King over all powers. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The ascension and enthronement place the risen Christ above all heavenly authorities in the divine council (cf. Eph 1:20-21; Phil 2:9-11; Heb 1:3, 13).

verses.flow: And those, having gone out, proclaimed everywhere, the Lord working with them and confirming the word through the accompanying signs.⟧
verses.note: GREEK: "Synergountos" (συνεργοῦντος, G4903) — "working together with" — indicates the Lord's ongoing active partnership with his apostles. "Bebaiountos" (βεβαιοῦντος, G950) means "confirming" or "establishing as legally valid." THEOLOGICAL: The signs (sēmeiōn, G4592) serve not as ends in themselves but as divine confirmation that the proclaimed word carries the authority of the enthroned Lord. The Gospel thus closes not with a static ending but with an open, expanding mission.
